flashcards.Back: A connection-oriented transport layer protocol that provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of a stream of data. it is used by applications like HTTP, FTP, and SSH.
A connectionless transport layer protocol that provides a simple, unreliable message service. It prioritizes speed and low overhead, making it ideal for real-time applications like VoIP.
A communication model that requires a pre-established session between two endpoints before data can be transferred. TCP uses a three-way handshake to achieve this.
A communication model where data is sent without first establishing a dedicated session. UDP operates this way, allowing for faster transmission but with no delivery guarantees.
The ability of a protocol to ensure that all data segments arrive at the destination intact. TCP provides this through acknowledgments and retransmissions.
A mechanism that prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver. TCP uses windowing to manage the amount of data that can be sent before an acknowledgment is required.
A dynamic flow control technique where the receiver specifies the number of bytes it is currently able to accept. The sender adjusts its transmission rate based on this value.
A TCP feature that ensures segments are delivered to the application in the same order they were sent. The receiver uses sequence numbers to rearrange out-of-order packets.
A process at the transport layer that allows multiple applications on a single host to share one network connection. it uses unique port numbers to distinguish between data streams.
The Protocol Data Unit (PDU) at the transport layer for TCP. it consists of a 20-byte header followed by the application data.
The PDU at the transport layer for UDP. it consists of a minimal 8-byte header and is sent with 'best-effort' delivery.
A 16-bit field in both TCP and UDP headers that identifies the sending application. For client requests, it is typically an ephemeral port number.
A 16-bit field that identifies the intended receiving application or service on the destination host, such as port 80 for a web server.
A 32-bit field in the TCP header used to track the order of data bytes. it allows the receiver to detect missing data and reassemble the stream correctly.
A TCP field used to inform the sender that data was received. it indicates the sequence number of the next byte the receiver expects to get.
Individual bits in the TCP header used to control the state of a connection. Important flags include SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, PSH, and URG.
A field in both TCP and UDP headers used for error detection. The receiver recalculates the value and discards the segment if it does not match the original.
The TCP flag used to initiate a connection. it is the first packet sent in the three-way handshake process.
A TCP flag used to confirm the receipt of a segment. it is used in almost every packet after the initial SYN request.
The TCP flag used to gracefully terminate a connection. Both sides of a session must send and acknowledge a FIN packet to close the link.
The combination of an IP address and a port number. it uniquely identifies a specific communication endpoint for an application across the network.
A temporary port number (range 49152–65535) automatically assigned by a client's OS for a single session. it is used as the source port for outgoing requests.
The process TCP uses to establish a session: Client sends SYN, Server responds with SYN-ACK, and Client sends ACK.
The delivery model used by UDP where the network makes no guarantee that data will arrive. if a datagram is lost, UDP does not attempt to retransmit it.
A TCP mechanism where the sender retransmits any data segments that were not acknowledged by the receiver within a specific timeout period.
The extra data required by a protocol header. UDP has very low overhead (8 bytes), while TCP has higher overhead (20 bytes) to support its complex features.
The well-known port used for HTTPS (Secure Web Browsing). it requires the reliability and connection management features of TCP.
The standard port used for DNS queries. UDP is preferred for these small, single-packet requests to minimize latency and network traffic.
The well-known port used for SSH (Secure Shell). it relies on TCP to ensure that every management command is delivered reliably and in order.
The standard port used by SNMP managers to query agents. UDP is used because network management should not add excessive overhead or rely on a complex session.
